text: The surgeon performs an incision in the right flank, which will later serve as the site for the ileostomy.
The surgeon inserts an eleven millimeter trocar into the surgical field, preparing for further laparoscopic access.
A ten millimeter, thirty-degree rigid scope is inserted into the surgical field, providing a clear view of the internal anatomy.
The surgeon places a six millimetre flexible trocar at the six o'clock position, complementing the previously inserted trocars for the procedure.
A bi-curved grasping forceps is introduced through the flexible trocar positioned at twelve o'clock, visible within the surgical field.
A monocurve grasping forceps is introduced through the flexible trocar positioned at the six o'clock location.
The surgeon positions the greater omentum and small bowel above the right liver, organizing the abdominal contents for optimal exposure during the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon inserts a monocurve coagulating hook through a flexible trocar positioned at six o'clock, preparing for tissue coagulation in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the origin of the inferior mesenteric vein using a monocurve coagulating hook, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
A straight 5mm clipper plier is introduced through the flexible trocar at the six o'clock position to clip the identified inferior mesenteric vein.
Monocurve scissors are introduced through the flexible trocar at a six o'clock position, and the vein is cut.
The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon, carefully following the anatomical plane and respecting Toldt's fascia, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon frees the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin and secures it with 5mm clips, using precise clipping techniques.
The surgeon uses monocurve scissors to cut the inferior mesenteric artery, following its previous dissection and clipping.
The surgeon continues to free the left mesocolon, maintaining dissection along the anatomical plane while respecting Toldt's fascia.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectosigmoid colon from medial to lateral, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon frees the sigmoid and left colon from the lateral parietal wall, completing the mobilization of these sections.
The surgeon dissects the girotofascia from the left mesocolon, carefully separating these anatomical structures.
The surgeon sections the left mesocolon intercorporeally using a monocurve coagulating hook and secures the tissue with five millimeter clips.
The surgeon inserts the monocurve needle holder through the flexible trocar positioned at the six o'clock orientation.
Above the pelvic bone, the surgeon introduces a straight needle percutaneously into the cavity, retracting the uterus to provide better access to the surgical site.
The surgeon uses Kelly Graspers to externally clamp the suture, securing it outside the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision, carefully dissecting the mesorectum from surrounding tissues within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon identifies the presacral fascia and carefully respects the tissue, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon performs the total mesorectal excision (TME) posteriorly, focusing on the area behind the rectum.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, initially mobilizing the left side of the rectal area, continuing the total mesorectal excision procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum anteriorly, carefully preserving the attachments to the vagina during the dissection.
The surgeon performs the total mesorectal excision by dissecting downward and laterally on the left side, continuing the mobilization of the rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly, carefully maintaining a position anterior to the presacral fascia.
The levator ani muscle on the left side is clearly visible during the dissection.
A percutaneous suture is introduced and placed in the peritoneal sheath, visibly covering the vaginal area.
The surgeon increases the exposure of the operative field, enhancing visibility of the anatomical structures involved in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon isolates the inferior rectal artery posterior to the rectum, then clips and divides the artery, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) posteriorly, ensuring both levator ani muscles are preserved and visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon returns to anterior dissection, mobilizing the rectum from the vagina and laterally, with visible separation of these structures.
The surgeon selects the level for the rectal section, preparing to make a precise incision at the chosen site.

The surgeon introduces a five millimeter scope into the flexible trocar positioned at six o'clock. The eleven millimeter trocar is replaced by a thirteen millimeter trocar to facilitate the introduction of a linear stapler.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler with a blue load to divide the inferior rectum, completing the transection of this section.
The surgeon grasps the rectum and removes it through the single axis, completing the resection process.
The surgeon removes the central trocar and the flexible trocar positioned at six o'clock, then joins the fascia together at the surgical site.
A plastic protection device is introduced into the surgical field, and the specimen is carefully extracted from the rectal area.
The surgeon cuts the left colon and introduces the anvil of the circular stapler into the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a purse string suture on the rectal stump, preparing it for anastomosis.
The anvil of the circular stapler is pushed into the abdominal cavity, following the removal of the rectum through the single axis.
The surgeon partially closes the peritoneal sheet and anterior rectifacia, using sutures to approximate the tissue layers within the surgical field.
The surgeon reinserts the 11mm trocar into the abdominal cavity, preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon inserts the circular stapler transanally and performs the colorectal mechanical anastomosis, visible under laparoscopic view.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis, visually inspecting for any signs of leakage under laparoscopic view.
The surgeon closes the mesocolic window, beginning with the closure of the pelvic peritoneal sheet, using sutures to secure the tissue layers.
The surgeon continues suturing until reaching the first arjunal loop, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon repositions the entire small bowel into its natural anatomical position within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon searches for the ileocecal valve and selects an ileal loop located twenty centimeters from it.
The surgeon removes the trocars and prepares the selected segment of the small bowel for a temporary ileostomy at the level of the single incision.
